Autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency -current diagnostic approaches and future perspectives

evidence_entities.text: The adrenal glands are small endocrine glands located on top of each kidney, producing hormones regulating important functions in our body like metabolism and stress.
There are several underlying causes for adrenal insufficiency, where an autoimmune attack by the immune system is the most common cause.

The adrenal cortex is responsible for producing glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and adrenal androgens whereas the medulla produces adrenaline and noradrenaline, also referred to as catecholamines
Glucocorticoids, primarily cortisol, also known as the stress hormone, have numerous effects on metabolism.
Mineralocorticoids, primarily aldosterone, maintain salt-water balance and regulate blood pressure
Primary AI (PAI), also known as Addison's disease, is a rare disease with a prevalence of approximately 100 per million in Europe
The most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency is autoimmunity; this type is often called Autoimmune Addison's Disease, or simply autoimmune PAI.
Morning cortisol concentration <140 nmol/L and an ACTH concentration twice the upper reference limit verifies a PAI diagnosis
The standard medication of glucocorticoid treatment for patients with AI (primary and secondary) is hydrocortisone or cortisone acetate
Patients with primary AI additionally take fludrocortisone treatment for mineralocorticoid deficiency.
A number of genes are known to confer early onset adrenal disease in monogenic inheritance patterns, including genetic defects in enzymes of adrenal steroidogenesis (e.g., CYP21A1 and CYP11A1)
We recently performed the world's first GWAS on autoimmune PAI including only ~1200 patients, but which succeeded to elucidate nine genetic regions with quite remarkable risk potential for PAI
The autoantibodies targeting 21-OH was first described in 1992 by Winqvist et al., identified as a major autoantigen in autoimmune PAI
These antibodies have since then been shown to be highly specific for autoimmune PAI in several patient cohorts
For T1D, the FDA recently approved teplizumab as the first disease-modifying therapy in type 1 diabetes.

properties.note: autoimmune PAI is the most common form of PAI

notes: This review paper provides comprehensive coverage of autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency, including diagnostic approaches, genetic factors, and future therapeutic perspectives.
The paper emphasizes the role of 21-hydroxylase autoantibodies as key diagnostic markers for autoimmune PAI.
Multiple genetic risk factors are discussed, including both monogenic and polygenic inheritance patterns.
